hatch_conditions: General mayfly attractor; works when fish are rising but hatch is unclear. One of the most versatile dry flies in existence.
Overcast days, cooler temps, small Baetis mayfly hatches. One of the most widespread and important hatches across the country.
tying_keywords: BWO dry fly tying parachute sparkle dun

hatch_conditions: Evening caddis activity, moderate to fast flows. Works dead-drifted or skated across the surface.
Warmer water temps, clear water, morning to midday PMD mayfly hatches. Especially important in Rocky Mountain and Western rivers.
tying_keywords: PMD dry fly tying sparkle dun

hatch_conditions: Stonefly and caddis hatches, grasshopper season. Also works as an attractor or dry/dropper indicator fly in fast water.
Stonefly season, terrestrial feeding, high water. Floats high and can support a dropper nymph. Great attractor for aggressive fish.
tying_keywords: Chubby Chernobyl foam dry fly tying

hatch_conditions: Grasshopper season (July–September), near grassy banks and meadow streams. Great dry/dropper indicator fly.
tying_keywords: Parachute hopper terrestrial dry fly tying

hatch_conditions: Midge cluster imitation. Key pattern when trout are sipping tiny insects in slow water or tailwaters. Works year-round.
tying_keywords: Griffith's Gnat midge dry fly tying

hatch_conditions: One of the most important early-season hatches in the Northeast. Occurs when water reaches 50–55°F, typically April–May. Key pattern on Catskill and Pennsylvania streams.
Late spring into summer evening hatches. Critical pattern on Northeastern limestone streams and Pennsylvania spring creeks. Also relevant in Appalachia.
One of the most dramatic hatches of the year — large mayflies bring big fish to the surface. Key event on Pennsylvania's Penns Creek and many Northeastern rivers.
tying_keywords: Green Drake dry fly tying mayfly

hatch_conditions: Tiny mayfly spinner fall, typically early mornings in late summer. Trout can become extremely selective. Important across the Northeast and tailwaters.
Early spring mayfly hatch common in the Northeast and Mid-Atlantic. One of the first big hatches of the season on freestone streams.
tying_keywords: March Brown dry fly tying mayfly

hatch_conditions: Classic attractor pattern when specific hatch is unknown. Works in most trout waters nationwide.
Terrestrial ant falls, especially after rain or wind. Trout eat ants consistently throughout summer. Often underutilized by anglers.
tying_keywords: Foam ant terrestrial dry fly tying

hatch_conditions: Year-round subsurface mayfly imitation. Works in most flows and depths. One of the most universally effective nymphs.
Buggy, versatile nymph imitating mayflies, caddis larvae, and scuds. Works in fast riffles and slow runs. A go-to searching pattern.
Classic stonefly and attractor nymph. Flashy appearance draws fish in fast water and deep runs. One of the most versatile nymphs.
Heavy, fast-sinking nymph for deep presentations. Imitates stoneflies and mayflies. Excellent dropper fly under a dry or indicator.
Essential tailwater and cold-water midge larva/pupa pattern. Critical in winter when midge hatches dominate. Works in slow pools and deep runs.
Flashy midge and mayfly emerger. Works when nothing else does — particularly effective on tailwaters and selective fish.
tying_keywords: Rainbow Warrior nymph tying midge emerger

hatch_conditions: Large stonefly nymph imitation. Key during runoff and high water when fish need a big target. Also imitates crane flies and crayfish.
tying_keywords: Pat's Rubber Legs stonefly nymph tying

hatch_conditions: Imitates aquatic worms dislodged by currents or rain. Highly effective in off-color water and pools. Works well on pressured fish.
Modern competition-style nymph with resin-coated body. Sinks fast in fast water. Works as a general mayfly and midge imitation in the water column.
Specific to tailwaters below reservoirs with Mysis shrimp populations (e.g., Fryingpan River, Blue Mesa). Can produce extremely large trout.
Active swimmer nymph found in fast currents of eastern rivers. Important hatch in the Northeast and Mid-Atlantic. Trout key in on both nymph and dun stages.
Large stonefly nymph for rocky western rivers. Fish year-round subsurface, especially productive before big stonefly hatches in spring.
Imitates trout, salmon, and sucker eggs during spawn seasons. Highly effective in rivers with spawning activity. Multiple color variations.
tying_keywords: Egg fly tying glo bug spawn

hatch_conditions: Universal streamer imitating baitfish, leeches, and large nymphs. Works in nearly all conditions and waters. Olive and black are most versatile colors.
Sculpin and baitfish imitation. Works well stripped, swung, or dead-drifted. Especially effective for large brown trout in the fall.
Jigging baitfish imitation with lead eyes for fast sinking. Versatile across rivers and lakes. Works for trout, bass, and pike.
Articulated sculpin imitation for large predatory trout. Designed to provoke reaction strikes from aggressive browns and rainbows in fast water.
Large streamer for targeting trophy-sized trout. Aggressive feeding periods and when larger prey is available.
Classic New England featherwing streamer. Excellent for landlocked Atlantic salmon and large brook trout in Maine and Vermont lakes and rivers.
tying_keywords: Grey Ghost streamer tying New England featherwing

hatch_conditions: Classic baitfish imitation for Northeastern streams. Developed by Art Flick for Catskill rivers. Imitates the common dace minnow found in most eastern trout streams.
tying_keywords: Black Nosed Dace streamer tying Catskill

hatch_conditions: Bright attractor streamer effective for landlocked salmon and large trout. Popular throughout New England and the Great Lakes region.
tying_keywords: Mickey Finn streamer tying landlocked salmon

hatch_conditions: Rabbit strip streamer with excellent pulsing action. Effective for large trout and bass. Works in a variety of flows and clarity conditions.
Mayfly and caddis emerger sitting in the surface film. Developed for Colorado's demanding tailwater trout. Works on selective fish during hatches.
Imitates caddis pupa rising to the surface during emergence. Dead-drifted or swung through runs. Critical during evening caddis activity.
Midge and small mayfly emerger. Slim, sparse profile. Extremely effective on South Platte and other Colorado tailwaters with picky, educated trout.
Swung through currents to imitate emerging insects. Extremely versatile — can imitate caddis pupae, mayfly emergers, or small baitfish. Classic and effective.
tying_keywords: Soft Hackle wet fly tying partridge

hatch_conditions: Critical stillwater pattern for mountain lakes and reservoirs. Active swimmer — fish with slow strips near weed beds and drop-offs.
Freshwater shrimp imitation. Key on spring creeks and tailwaters with cold, alkaline water. Orange, pink, and olive are most productive colors.
Chenille or microfiber grub imitation. Controversial but highly effective, especially in off-color water or when fish are lethargic. Works as a dropper under an indicator.
